summary: Filled all planning artifacts with concrete V1 scope: CLI tool scanning Express/Fastify/NestJS codebases, producing capability manifests, compiling tools, and serving via local MCP. Awaiting human approval to open planning gate.

decisions.statement: V1 is scoped to the discovery-manifest-compile-serve pipeline for 3 Node.js frameworks only. All 12 vision domains are deferred to V2+.
decisions.rationale: The vision is deliberately broad but V1 must ship a complete, testable vertical slice. Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of production Node.js API frameworks. Going deep on one ecosystem beats going shallow on many.

decisions.statement: MCP serve is describe-only in V1 — no live API proxying.
decisions.rationale: Live proxy requires auth forwarding, error normalization, and reliability handling. These are significant concerns that would delay V1. Describe-only MCP still proves the pipeline end-to-end and is useful for agent discovery.

decisions.statement: Capability grouping starts as 1:1 route-to-capability mapping. Intelligent grouping deferred.
decisions.rationale: Smart capability grouping (merging related endpoints into higher-level capabilities) is the core vision differentiator but requires heuristic tuning against real codebases. Starting 1:1 lets us ship and iterate based on real feedback.

decisions.statement: No plugin API in V1. Framework support is hardcoded for Express, Fastify, NestJS.
decisions.rationale: Premature abstraction of plugin interfaces risks building the wrong API. V1 hardcodes 3 frameworks; the plugin boundary emerges from the patterns we discover during implementation.

decisions.statement: TypeScript/JavaScript ecosystem only in V1. Python, Go, Java are V2+.
decisions.rationale: Single AST parser ecosystem (ts-morph or equivalent) lets us go deep on inference quality. Multi-language support multiplies the scanning surface without adding core product value.

verification.evidence_summary: All 4 planning artifacts verified to exist with non-zero content. PM_SIGNOFF.md (3979 bytes), SYSTEM_SPEC.md (10093 bytes), ROADMAP.md (4179 bytes), command-surface.md (2201 bytes).

summary: Fixed 4 contradictions in planning artifacts: tusq review is non-interactive (not interactive TUI), manifest requires explicit prior scan (no implicit scan), approval mechanism documented (manual JSON edit), and added missing error case for no scan data.

decisions.statement: tusq review is non-interactive in V1 — prints summary to stdout only. Users approve capabilities by editing tusq.manifest.json directly. Interactive TUI deferred to V1.1.
decisions.rationale: Previous artifacts contradicted themselves: scope item 11 said 'interactive review/approval' but behavior section said 'prints to stdout'. V1 has no TUI — keeping it simple and consistent.

decisions.statement: tusq manifest requires an explicit prior tusq scan — no implicit scan fallback in V1.
decisions.rationale: Implicit scan is hidden complexity that makes the pipeline harder to reason about. Explicit steps are clearer for V1. Users run tusq scan then tusq manifest.

summary: Fixed scan data persistence gap (added .tusq/scan.json file contract) and clarified --format json on scan. All planning artifacts are internally consistent and ready for human signoff.

decisions.statement: Scan data persists to .tusq/scan.json as an internal file contract between tusq scan and tusq manifest.
decisions.rationale: The spec defined an implicit dependency between scan and manifest but never specified the intermediate file. Without this, an implementer would have to invent the storage location, risking divergence from spec intent.

summary: Addressed injected planning revision intent: added minimal domain grouping to V1 scope, clarified tusq serve proves MCP exposure not executable delivery, and framed runtime learning as a deferred core pillar across all planning artifacts.

decisions.statement: V1 includes automatic domain grouping — capabilities are grouped by resource name or controller (e.g., /users/* routes → 'users' domain). Cross-resource merging deferred to V1.1.
decisions.rationale: VISION.md mandates 'basic skill composition: domain grouping for generated tools' in V1. Pure 1:1 with no grouping contradicted the vision. Minimal domain assignment adds organizational value without complex heuristics.

decisions.statement: All planning docs now explicitly state V1 proves discovery + manifest + MCP exposure, not full executable capability delivery. tusq serve is describe-only.
decisions.rationale: Human feedback required honest product claims. V1 does not proxy live API calls — stating this clearly prevents overpromising and aligns expectations for V1.1 proxy work.

decisions.statement: Runtime learning is documented as a deferred core pillar of the tusq.dev vision, not a normal omitted feature. It is planned for V2.
decisions.rationale: Runtime instrumentation and production-signal-driven manifest refinement are foundational to the vision (the bridge from 'good enough' to 'continuously accurate'). Treating it as a minor omission misrepresented its importance.

summary: Revalidated implementation on the updated baseline, closed CLI flag-surface gaps, and passed smoke verification.

decisions.statement: Rejected prior attempt assumptions and revalidated against current HEAD after operator baseline drift before claiming readiness.
decisions.rationale: Attempt 1 was rejected because baseline changed post-dispatch; proceeding without rebasing verification would risk false acceptance evidence.

decisions.statement: Aligned CLI behavior with planned command surface by accepting `--verbose` across commands and printing command help on invalid flags.
decisions.rationale: This removes a concrete UX/spec mismatch and hardens user-error handling with explicit, test-covered guidance.

commands: node bin/tusq.js scan --bad-flag >/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out 2>/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.err; code=$?; test $code -eq 1; grep -q "Usage: tusq scan" /tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out
verification.evidence_summary: Smoke test suite passed end-to-end, and a wrapped negative-case check confirmed invalid flags return exit 1 while printing command usage help.

summary: Re-baselined QA from scratch against post-checkpoint HEAD (attempt 2); all 22 acceptance criteria and smoke suite verified passing; ship verdict updated and all gate artifacts confirmed.

decisions.statement: Rejected attempt-1 QA artifacts as stale and re-ran all verification from scratch against current HEAD to close the baseline drift that caused attempt-1 rejection.
decisions.rationale: The previous attempt was invalidated because an implementation checkpoint commit landed after QA was dispatched, making its evidence unreliable. Correct QA discipline requires re-running all checks against the actual HEAD rather than trusting prior evidence.

decisions.statement: Ship verdict remains SHIP. No new issues were found in the re-verification pass; the implementation matches the V1 contract (DEC-001 through DEC-011) and all 22 acceptance criteria pass.
decisions.rationale: Smoke test suite exited 0; manual CLI spot-checks for help, version, unknown command, invalid flag, and per-command --verbose all behaved as specified. The implementation changes introduced in the dev checkpoint (--verbose flag acceptance and command usage on invalid flags) are correct and covered by the test suite.

commands: node bin/tusq.js scan --bad-flag >/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out 2>/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.err; code=$?; test $code -eq 1; grep -q 'Usage: tusq scan' /tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out
verification.evidence_summary: Smoke test suite (node tests/smoke.mjs) exited 0, covering all 22 requirements across all 6 commands and 3 frameworks. Manual checks confirmed: --help exits 0 with 8-command listing; version prints 0.1.0; unknown command exits 1 with 'Unknown command:' error; invalid flag exits 1 with command usage on stdout. All negative-case checks were wrapped in verifiers that exit 0 only when the failure occurs as expected.

machine_evidence.command: node bin/tusq.js scan --bad-flag >/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out 2>/tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.err; code=$?; test $code -eq 1 && grep -q 'Usage: tusq scan' /tmp/tusq-invalid-flag.out

summary: Replaced placeholder launch artifacts with V1-accurate messaging and aligned README/homepage copy to the verified tusq.dev v0.1.0 story.

decisions.statement: Rejected the assumption implied by the prior QA ship verdict that launch readiness followed automatically; the required launch artifacts were still placeholders and public-facing entry points overstated V1.
decisions.rationale: QA validated product behavior, not market narrative. Shipping with placeholder launch docs or vision-heavy copy would have created immediate expectation drift.

decisions.statement: Positioned tusq.dev v0.1.0 as an open-source capability compiler CLI for Express, Fastify, and NestJS that delivers scan, manifest, approval, compile, review, and describe-only MCP serve.
decisions.rationale: This matches the verified command surface, supported frameworks, and QA-confirmed behavior without importing deferred roadmap claims.

decisions.statement: Aligned the four required launch artifacts plus the README and homepage around explicit defendable claims and non-claims.
decisions.rationale: Consistent entry-point messaging reduces overclaim risk, especially around live execution, runtime learning, framework coverage, and review UX.

commands: for f in .planning/MESSAGING.md .planning/LAUNCH_PLAN.md .planning/CONTENT_CALENDAR.md .planning/ANNOUNCEMENT.md; do if grep -n 'TBD' "$f"; then exit 1; fi; done
verification.evidence_summary: Re-ran the smoke suite to ensure the documentation-only launch edits did not affect product behavior, verified the edited files are free of diff-check formatting issues, and confirmed the four required launch artifacts no longer contain placeholder TBD content.
